data.title: How has Hong Kong-Singapore Asian Cup football qualifier rekindled scalping issue?
data.description:
The highly anticipated Asian Cup qualifying match between Hong Kong and Singapore at Kai Tak Stadium next Tuesday is the latest mega-event in the city targeted by scalpers, some of whom were found reselling tickets at 17 times the official price.
Disappointed fans found that the match sold out in less than 80 minutes last Friday, but discovered listings on sites including online marketplace Carousell. One seller offered three tickets in the upper-level fan zone for HK$4,000 (US$515) – nearly 17 times the original total price of HK$240.
Hong Kong’s legislation on the unauthorised resale of tickets, the Places of Public Entertainment Ordinance, was introduced decades ago to deal with gangs who touted tickets in person, but fails to regulate today’s predominant system of online sales.
The Post takes a look at how the critical game triggered a fresh round of ticket scalping in Hong Kong.
What is the outcry about?
Tickets for the match sold out in less than 80 minutes last Friday after going on sale at 3pm via the online platform Ticketflap, with no real-name verification required.
Customers could buy up to six tickets in each transaction.
Fans were further angered when listings appeared on sites including online marketplace Carousell, raising concerns that scalpers had snapped up large numbers of tickets.
One scalper offered three tickets in the upper-level fan zone for HK$4,000 (US$515) – nearly 17 times the original total price of HK$240.
Supporters flooded the Hong Kong Football Association’s (HKFA) social media pages to complain about being unable to buy tickets, with some calling the arrangement “chaotic” and “unfair”.
Why is this match important?
Victory would send the Hong Kong team to the 2027 AFC Asian Cup finals in Saudi Arabia, while defeat would end their qualification hopes.
A draw would mean both teams having to fight on in a final round of qualifiers next March.
The HKFA on Wednesday upped the stakes by offering the team a HK$1 million (US$128,700) “reward” if they defeated Singapore.

What are the developments so far?
Following the backlash, HKFA chief executive Charles Cheung Yim-yau said on Thursday it was “not their responsibility” to police ticket sales.
He said that the speed at which the game had sold out meant “our matches are very popular”.
Cheung added that HKFA listened to fans’ feedback, citing the fact that each customer could only buy six tickets for the Singapore match, after complaints over people being able to buy eight for the last qualifier against Bangladesh in October.
Faced with sky-high demand, some Hong Kong fans registered through the Football Association of Singapore (FAS) to secure seats through its ticketing partner Ticketek in the away fans’ area, saying it was the only way they could get a ticket.
In response to media queries, an FAS spokesman said it is aware of recent reports on the “resale and misuse of away tickets”.
The FAS said it would require fans to show a valid Singapore photo ID or passport that matches the name of the purchaser during ticket collection.
Ticket holders would be required to sign an “on-site fan declaration” that they would support Singapore only and refrain from wearing home team apparel or displaying home team paraphernalia.
Is scalping illegal in Hong Kong?
Under the Public Entertainment Ordinance, it is an offence to offer or sell tickets at higher prices than those fixed by organisers at licensed venues. The unauthorised sale of tickets – even at official prices – is also outlawed “in any public thoroughfare, or in the entrance hall of, or approaches to” the venue.
Offenders face a fine of up to HK$2,000 - a penalty that has remained unchanged since 1950.
But while criminalising in-person touting at entertainment venues, the law does not cover online scalping, which has plagued the city in an age of internet sales.
The Culture, Sports and Tourism Bureau previously told the legislature it was studying possible measures including new legislation to criminalise ticket scalping for events held at Leisure and Cultural Services Department venues.
But it maintained that “legislative control has its pros and cons”, given the multiple causes of scalping, including supply and demand.
Why is there a market for scalpers?
In 2019, the department announced that organisers of events with more than four shows at its venues must limit internal and priority sales to no more than 70 per cent, leaving only 30 per cent for public sales.
While venues managed by the government must follow the guidelines, private ones such as those at Kai Tak Sports Park and AsiaWorld-Expo can decide their own sales terms.
Event industry insiders told the Post earlier that commercial interests meant that organisers were contractually obliged to reserve up to 60 per cent of tickets for their sponsors’ internal distribution or sales through other channels as part of their contract.
Secretary for Culture, Sports and Tourism Rosanna Law Shuk-pui previously said her bureau had met several major organisers to understand their perspectives.
But she noted that events were commercial activities and “need to be financially viable”, while large-scale productions required a certain level of sponsorship.
“It is not something government action can easily change. As long as it’s not illegal, the government’s hand should not be too far-reaching as Hong Kong is a free market,” she said.

Is real name registration a solution?
The Cathay/HSBC Hong Kong Sevens, which welcomed almost 50,000 fans on each of its three days this year, uses real-name registration for ticket sales.
The system has also been deployed for the ongoing National Games, in which Hong Kong is staging events in eight sports.
In response to the outrage from football fans, Cheung, the HKFA CEO, defended his organisation’s sales mechanism, saying it did not need to apologise for the backlash and that it was “not their responsibility” to police ticket sales.
Cheung said having to verify fans’ identity on match night could cause delays, and that after “cautious consideration”, the HKFA had decided that real-name registration risked “creating other big issues”.
But he acknowledged that there were no measures in place to stop unscrupulous people from buying tickets and reselling them at inflated prices.
Culture, sports and tourism minister Law previously said that while real-name ticketing was a proven solution, the government acknowledged that many event organisers were reluctant to adopt the system out of business concerns.

data.title: Hongkongers told to brace for chilly weather early next week due to monsoon
data.description:
This story has been made freely available as a public service to our readers. Please consider supporting SCMP’s journalism by subscribing.
Temperatures in Hong Kong are expected to dip to as low as 13 degrees Celsius (55.4 Fahrenheit) next Wednesday due to a powerful surge caused by a northeast monsoon approaching the coast of Guangdong, according to the local forecaster.
The Hong Kong Observatory said on Thursday that an “intense replenishment” of the northeast monsoon was expected to strike the coast of Guangdong next Monday night, resulting in cooler and windy weather in the region for several days.
The city would also experience dry and warm weather with sunny periods this weekend, before a major shift next Monday as winds strengthen from the north to northeast, reaching offshore and high ground by night, and leading to a noticeable drop in temperatures.
According to the latest nine-day forecast, the minimum temperature will stand at 15 degrees on Tuesday next week, before dipping to as low as 13 degrees the day after.
But residents can expect higher temperatures and sunny periods in the latter half of the week, as the monsoon is forecast to become more moderate, with daytime mercury readings reaching 24 degrees and the minimum temperature sitting at 19 degrees.

data.title: Hong Kong urged to draw on overseas models in supporting ‘meaningful’ retirements
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities should look to overseas jurisdictions such as Canada and Japan for policies that can help the city’s elderly lead more meaningful lives in retirement, a local think tank has said, describing current measures as too “reactive”.
In a new report released on Thursday, Our Hong Kong Foundation also called for greater age inclusiveness in the private sector, noting that most support for retirees currently came from public or government-subsidised groups.
“Although retired adults in Hong Kong are living longer, they are not necessarily living healthier or happier,” the think tank said.
“We propose a vision where retired adults are financially secure, connected to their communities, and empowered to contribute. This requires a collaborative, multi-actor effort to provide a full spectrum of services that meet diverse needs.”
As Hong Kong continues to grapple with a rapidly ageing population, the government has introduced support measures for this at-risk group while also seeking to tap into the so-called “silver economy”.
In his latest policy address, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu pledged to allocate HK$500 million (US$64.2 million) annually in recurrent expenditure to support carers.
The funding will include initiatives such as establishing a high-risk family database and installing accident detection systems.
The think tank identified several key barriers preventing retirees from enjoying a fulfilling retirement, including difficulty maintaining a positive emotional outlook, financial insecurity, and the pressures of caregiving responsibilities.
Its report drew on 17 studies conducted by academics, healthcare providers and NGOs, alongside 15 interviews with retired Hongkongers.

It cited recent findings showing that while retirees were living longer, many of their additional years were spent coping with physical and cognitive challenges, while levels of happiness were declining.
There had been a “clear evolution” in the government’s approach to retirement support, shifting from a needs-based safety net towards a more “holistic strategy” focused on empowerment, the foundation said.
However, it argued that the current strategy still leaned too heavily towards reactive policymaking that responded to issues such as bed shortages in elderly care homes, or pursued what it described as “trendy topics” like the silver economy.
Among the overseas policies the foundation recommended for consideration was the Ontario Senior Achievement Award, introduced in the Canadian province in 1986, which recognises seniors who have made “significant contributions” to their communities.
It also highlighted Japan’s development of its “silver economy”, noting that the Japanese government had shifted from a state-centred model to one involving greater private sector participation.
The think tank assessed services used by older adults based on the presence of private-sector involvement and their degree of age inclusiveness – such as the use of larger fonts or tailored support.
It found age inclusiveness to be highest in public and private healthcare-related services, including district elderly community centres, hospitals and family doctors.
By contrast, inclusiveness in areas related to leisure, enjoyment and self-worth was described as “significantly underdeveloped”.
Sectors with strong private-sector involvement, including gyms, property developers, hotels, food and drink, and financial and legal services, were singled out as lacking age inclusiveness.
“Although a diverse range of services for retired adults is offered, age inclusiveness has yet to gain mainstream adoption within the private sector’s offerings,” it said.

data.title: Juan Ponce Enrile, architect of Philippine martial law and People Power, dies at 101: daughter
data.description:
Juan Ponce Enrile, a shrewd political operator who helped usher in the darkest repression of the late Philippine dictator Ferdinand Marcos Snr’s rule, died on Thursday at the age of 101, his daughter said.
Enrile, who was being treated for pneumonia, died at home at 4.21pm “surrounded by our family”, Katrina Ponce Enrile said on her Facebook page, adding there would be a public viewing.
Known as the architect of the brutal martial law used to crush opposition to Marcos’ rule, Harvard-educated Enrile was a long-time top adviser to the authoritarian leader.
Enrile later turned on Marcos and was instrumental in sparking the popular 1986 uprising that led to the president’s ouster less than three years after the 1983 murder of opposition leader Benigno Aquino.
True to his survivor instincts, Enrile switched sides again and helped the Marcos family in their remarkable political comeback after they returned to the Philippines from exile.
After Ferdinand Marcos Jnr, the late dictator’s son, won the 2022 presidential election, he named Enrile his chief legal counsel, two years shy of his 100th birthday.
A wily politician, Enrile never spent a day behind bars for his role in the Marcos dictatorship despite its human rights abuses.
Instead, he enjoyed a decades-long career as a lawmaker.

A good beginning sours
According to his memoir, Enrile was a widow’s son by a prominent married lawyer. He was born Juanito Furagganan on February 14, 1924.
The boy later took his father’s name and became a respected lawyer himself, as well as a close confidant of rising politician Marcos, who would win the presidency in 1965.
Before becoming defence minister, Enrile held other key posts, including customs chief and justice minister.
Marcos’s order implementing martial law in 1972 cited various acts of terror around the country, including an alleged communist guerrilla assassination attempt on Enrile.
Many years later, Enrile gave differing statements on that key event, saying at one point the claimed ambush was made up, and then writing in his memoir that it had actually happened.
Under martial law, Enrile was the second most powerful man in the country, deciding who could be jailed or freed.

Amnesty International estimates Marcos’s security forces either killed, tortured, sexually abused, mutilated or arbitrarily detained tens of thousands of opponents.
Under legislation signed in 2013 by former president Benigno Aquino, son and namesake of the opposition leader assassinated 30 years earlier, more than 11,000 victims have been officially recognised and compensated.
Enrile never apologised for his role in the dictatorship and even defended martial law.
“It was operating well at least from 1972 all the way to 1975, but somehow along the way, just like everything that we do in this country, a good beginning sours,” he told journalists in 2006.
People Power
By the 1980s, Marcos was ailing, and it was becoming clear that his wife Imelda and her allies were not planning to keep Enrile in the future regime.
In response, Enrile organised a cabal of disgruntled young military officers into the Reform the Armed Forces Movement (RAM), laying plans to seize power.
The 1983 murder of Aquino by soldiers loyal to Marcos and massive cheating in the 1986 polls caused widespread unrest, further weakening Marcos’s position.
The RAM sought to exploit the situation with a plot to overthrow the Marcos government, but their plan was discovered before it could be launched.
Facing arrest, Enrile and his allies holed up at the military headquarters in Manila and appealed to the public to protect them from an imminent government attack.

Millions of people poured onto the streets in response, triggering the “People Power” revolt that toppled Marcos, installed Aquino’s widow Corazon as president, and restored democracy.
In gratitude for his role in Marcos’s downfall, Aquino appointed Enrile as her defence minister, but he stayed in her cabinet for only a short time.
He was thrice briefly arrested, in 1990, 2001, and 2014, the first two times for alleged involvement in coup plots and the last over the embezzlement of public funds.
All the while, Enrile remained an influential figure, serving as a lawmaker for most of the period, including as Senate president from 2008 to 2013.
In 2014, Enrile was arrested with two other senators for allegedly receiving bribes as part of a massive corruption scandal in which hundreds of millions of dollars in public funds intended for development projects were pilfered.
But while his co-accused languished behind bars, the Supreme Court, citing Enrile’s “fragile health”, granted him bail in 2015, allowing him to spend his twilight days at home.
A special graft court dismissed the plunder, or massive corruption, charges against Enrile last year, and last month acquitted him of the remaining graft cases against him as well.

data.title: China’s property sector needs stronger stimulus: Citigroup economist
data.description:
Stronger stimulus measures are needed to halt the decline in China’s embattled property sector, as Beijing aims to guide the national economy towards a stable and healthy trajectory, according to Citigroup.
An incentive package – including interest rate reductions, tax cuts, further relaxation of home purchase restrictions and the buy-back of idle land – could help revitalise the real estate industry, said Yu Xiangrong, Citi’s chief economist for Greater China, during a media briefing in Shanghai on Thursday.
“If Beijing targets a 5 per cent growth next year to sustain its economy, it has to expand domestic demand,” he said. “Efforts must be made to stabilise the property sector to encourage consumer spending.”
China’s economy expanded 5.2 per cent in the first three quarters of this year, making it likely to meet Beijing’s full-year growth target of 5 per cent. However, Yu predicted that the country’s gross domestic product would only rise 4.7 per cent next year.
Citi has joined the ranks of international banks advocating for more aggressive policies to support mainland China’s property market, which has been in a downward spiral since early 2022.
UBS and Goldman Sachs have also proposed measures to stabilise home prices and boost housing transactions as Beijing seeks to restore momentum to the world’s second-largest economy.
The property sector and related industries, such as home appliances and construction materials, account for about a quarter of the country’s economic output.
However, budget-conscious consumers have shied away from buying homes over the past three years, anticipating further declines in the market, which has been undermined by concerns over job prospects and income growth.

Falling home prices have also deterred mainland consumers – including the country’s 400 million middle-income residents, from freely spending on goods such as clothing and home appliances. That has affected consumption, which has long been a major growth driver for the Chinese economy.
A property market downturn was likely to continue, albeit with smaller contractions, said Zhang Ning, a senior economist at UBS, earlier this month.
“Faster implementation of structural measures and stronger policy support would boost domestic confidence and economic growth,” he said. However, the “property market trajectory is also hard to predict accurately in this downturn”.
S&P Global Ratings recently forecast that new home sales in China would decline by 8 per cent this year, followed by a 7 per cent decrease in 2026.
“My take is that government subsidies could be introduced to encourage homebuying,” Yu said. “Besides, tax incentives should be used to propel home sales.”
Several major Chinese cities have announced new policies this year to encourage home ownership.
In Shanghai, local residents can now own an unlimited number of flats outside the outer ring road, where two-thirds of the city’s housing is located. Previously, families were restricted to a maximum of two units.
In Beijing, both local and non-local residents can now freely purchase new and second-hand homes outside the Fifth Ring Road, a major highway encircling the suburbs.

data.title: 74.4% of Hongkongers say removing condoms without consent is sexual assault: survey
data.description:
More than 70 per cent of Hongkongers have said removing condoms without consent during intercourse is a form of sexual assault, while less than 1 per cent of surveyed victims have reported the matter to police, according to a poll by an NGO.
Executive director Doris Chong Tsz-wai of the Association Concerning Sexual Violence Against Women on Thursday urged authorities to create a statutory definition of consent to delineate the scope of protection for victims whose wishes were violated during sex.
She noted that other jurisdictions, such as Singapore, already had laws covering this type of act.
“The most important issue is in the vocabulary; if a victim could not describe what happened to them, it’s hard for them to identify that they had experienced sexual violence and subsequently seek help at relevant organisations,” Chong said.
The survey looked at “stealthing”, which refers to a sexual partner surreptitiously removing a condom during intercourse that was consensual under the promise of safe sex.
Under Hong Kong law, the legal responses to stealthing remain a grey area. The city has no dedicated laws criminalising the act, while the current legislation against rape and indecent assault may not be able to categorise this type of behaviour.
The association polled 277 people from October 2024 to February this year for the survey, with more than half of them aged between 18 and 34. The group consisted of women, men and non-binary people, encompassing various sexual orientations.
The survey found that 95.3 per cent of respondents deemed stealthing to be a violation of their sexual consent, while 74.4 per cent considered it to be sexual assault.
The poll revealed 57 respondents, or 20.6 per cent of those surveyed, had been victims of non-consensual condom removal during sex.
Respondents were also asked to weigh in on several other hypothetical scenarios.
The first case involved a sexual partner secretly piercing a condom during sex, with 95.7 per cent of respondents considering it to be a violation of consent and 74 per cent of those surveyed deeming the act to be a form of sexual assault.
In the second scenario, a partner ignores an agreement for safe sex by performing intercourse without any protection. The survey recorded that 93.9 per cent of respondents said the act violated consent, and 84.5 per cent considered it to be sexual assault.
Researchers also asked respondents to give their views on a scenario where a person experienced internal ejaculation without consent, with 78.3 per cent feeling it violated consent and 57.4 per cent deeming it to be sexual assault.
The NGO conducted in-depth interviews with 10 people of various genders and sexual orientations who had experienced stealthing before.
Albert Yau Chi-hang, a senior research officer with the association, said victims had reflected that sex with a condom and sex without one were fundamentally different acts that could not be consented to interchangeably.
“They think the level of intimacy and trust required between the two types of sex was markedly different. The sexual health risks associated with these two acts were also different,” he said.
In terms of the 57 victims’ most common responses to stealthing, 47.4 per cent responded by seeking testing for sexually transmitted diseases, while 40.4 per cent opted to take emergency contraceptive measures.
The survey also found that only two of the 57 victims had reported their cases to police.
One report was still under investigation, while police had ended their investigation into the other case without laying any charges.
Five respondents said they had sought help from an NGO, four had gone to friends and family and three had demanded compensation from the sexual partner involved.
Twelve said they had not taken any action.
Chong said the lack of a statutory definition of sexual consent in sexual offence laws could affect victims’ willingness to seek help.
“Current legal focus on whether the victim had consented to the sex act may affect victims,” she said.
“When they know that sex with and without condoms is different and that they consented to safe sex, this would help them delineate their feelings [after the incident].”
Chong said her association would call for the city government to clearly lay out the definition for sexual consent in the coming reform of sexual offence laws, including adopting an affirmative model where a person must clearly express consent for a sexual act to be deemed legal, to stamp out such acts.
“Our focus is on sexual consent. If we can establish the concept of consent clearly, that they know whether they had consented to the act, it would be easier to establish whether [a violation] was a sexual offence,” she said.

data.title: As ceasefire with Cambodia unravels, Thailand PM ‘no longer cares’ about tariffs
data.description:
Thailand and Cambodia traded blame on Thursday for the breakdown of the ceasefire over their disputed border, with Cambodian Prime Minister Hun Manet accusing Thai soldiers of killing a civilian, as villagers on both sides prepare for the conflict to return.
His Thai counterpart, Anutin Charnvirakul, on Wednesday risked incurring the wrath of US President Donald Trump by saying he “no longer cares” about the threat of tariffs, which were trimmed as a condition for the truce, as he accused Cambodia of flouting its side of the ceasefire deal, leaving him no option but to suspend the peace process.
On Tuesday, the Thai military, which has primacy over the civilian government in security matters, declared it would no longer take part in a four-point road map to peace after a soldier stepped on a landmine and lost a foot. It claimed that the landmine was planted on the Thai side of the border by Cambodian forces, prompting a denial by Phnom Penh.
“I followed every single step [of the peace deal], they didn’t,” Anutin said. “We can’t do that any more. As for trade and tariffs negotiations, I no longer care … if we can’t sell to this country, we’ll find others. How can we put our lives in the hands of one country?”
In a social media post, Hun Manet accused Thailand of firing across the border, killing one civilian and wounding several others on Wednesday.
The peace talks initiated by Trump last month in Kuala Lumpur were meant to end the conflict. Instead, Trump’s deal, dubbed the Kuala Lumpur Peace Accords and heralded by him as a chance to save “millions of lives” in both countries, appears to be on the verge of collapse.

The deal has been cosmetic and prone to collapse, with the rival militaries not prepared to back down due to the surging nationalistic feelings on both sides, according to analysts.
“It was predictable that the agreement would break down because it was plainly concluded to placate President Trump on matters unrelated to the conflict, namely trade and Trump’s desire to be perceived as a peacemaker,” said Matthew Wheeler, a senior analyst for Southeast Asia at International Crisis Group.
In July, dozens were killed on both sides in the five-day flare-up arising from an old grievance over their disputed border. Cambodia fired rockets deep into Thai territory, killing civilians, while Thailand responded with air sorties and missiles over the border.
Trump has used tariffs as a bargaining chip to help secure a ceasefire, offering both countries a flat rate of 19 per cent for their cooperation.
In Kuala Lumpur, Trump signed a trade deal with Cambodia, with Hun Manet repeating his intention to nominate the president for a Nobel Peace Prize, an accolade coveted by the US leader for years. Trump’s commerce officials are also making progress towards a trade deal with Thailand, which is expected to be signed in the coming months.


Along the Thai side of the border, villagers braced for worse to come, saying they could hear exchanges of small arms fire on Thursday, but were still farming as they sought to make up for income lost over recent months of tensions.
“Until it is rockets being fired, it is business as usual for us,” said a resident of Khantarak district on the Thai side of the border, who requested not to be named.
“I’ve been back to my rice paddy to harvest. We have to work, we have to eat. Both sides are doing this to provoke each other. I don’t believe in international diplomacy any more ... I think we have to fight until someone loses. Then we can end this.”

data.title: Baotou set to become China’s rare earth hub under Inner Mongolia’s 10-year plan
data.description:
A northern Chinese region that boasts the world’s biggest rare earth element (REE) mine is looking to develop its second-largest city, Baotou, into a world-leading hub for critical materials.
And analysts say the effort could further shore up China’s leading position in the supply chain.
In a 10-year development plan, which includes intentions to build a “solid ecological security barrier”, the Inner Mongolia autonomous region said it will make comprehensive progress on the protection and integrated use of critical minerals, launch a new round of strategic mine-exploration work, and accelerate the development of high-end rare earth functional materials.
The plan, issued on Wednesday and extending through 2035, says the application structure and spatial layouts for critical minerals will be optimised, and that the efficiency of resource utilisation will be enhanced.
Inner Mongolia is home to the Bayan Obo mine, which accounts for more than 40 per cent of the world’s known rare earth element reserves, and for nearly half of the global rare earth production, according to NS Energy Business, a news and analysis platform for the energy industry.
Rajiv Biswas, CEO of research firm Asia-Pacific Economics, said: “The strategic initiative to develop Baotou into an integrated rare earths supply-chain hub will further strengthen China’s position as the world’s REE producer.”
And he spoke to how “large-scale new investment of magnets using REE materials at Baotou will strengthen China’s global leadership in the production of magnets for new electric vehicles and wind turbines for renewable energy”.


In July, Xinhua reported that Chinese geologists had identified a large deposit in Inner Mongolia of a previously unknown rare earth mineral – a new carbonate mineral dominated by neodymium. The so-called magnet metal is used to build electric-vehicle motors and offshore wind turbines.
Along the rare earth value chain, China holds 69 per cent of the global share, in terms of mine production, according to recent figures from Morgan Stanley. And in terms of magnet supply, the investment bank’s analysts said China controls 90 per cent of the global market share.
Rare earths are a point of leverage for Beijing amid export controls on chips by the United States. And Cory Combs, associate director and head of critical minerals and supply-chain research at Trivium China, said in a podcast on Sunday that “the private sector has found ways to not be shut down by all this chaos”.
“[The] private sector has worked out ways to survive through various stockpiles with other countries, getting various less-mature production chains at a higher cost, but they’re still able to get it,” he added.
When asked about China’s rare earth export-licensing system during a regular press briefing on Thursday, Ministry of Commerce spokesman He Yadong said China conducts export controls on rare earths in accordance with laws and regulations.
“China is committed to safeguarding national security and interests,” he said, adding that efforts were being taken “to maintain the stability of the global supply chain”.
As many of the world’s largest developing countries transition to electric vehicles and renewable energy, Biswas said the integrated rare earth element supply-chain hub at Baotou will “play a key role” in supplying processed materials.
“While global REE supply chains will gradually become more diversified over the next five years, China will continue to play a dominant role in global REE supply chains over the medium-term outlook to 2030,” he added.

data.title: Dish in Focus: Aged rice duck à l’orange at Épure
data.description:
When you have a signature three-yellow-chicken dish as successful as chef Aven Lau’s at Épure, it’s usually a tough act to follow. And as Hong Kong’s chicken craze finally begins to plateau, the man who helped kick-start the trend has elegantly pivoted, turning his focus to a new muse: the Guangdong rice duck.
“I’m not French, so why force French ingredients?” Lau quips, a philosophy that crystallised early in his career. Though trained in French techniques, his culinary imagination remained fundamentally inspired by the flavours and principles of Chinese cuisine. “As I matured as a chef, I found many more interesting ways to cultivate this interest,” he explains. “I wouldn’t say that the food I prepare here is fusion food. Rather, we approach cooking with a sense of place, culture and time.”

This “aged rice duck à l’orange” represents the ultimate expression of that ethos – what Lau describes as “cooking closer to my roots as a Chinese person”, using ingredients that resonate with local palates. This philosophy begins with his selection of Guangdong rice duck, which Lau notes possesses a fundamental distinction from French varieties: Asian ducks are fully bled during processing, resulting in a “cleaner, less gamy” flavour that aligns perfectly with regional preferences.
Refining this duck took a seven-month odyssey of relentless experimentation. The development path involved tasting more than 80 ducks and spending countless hours researching techniques online, even on RedNote. “There were times when I thought I would never achieve the vision I had for the duck,” he admits. His solution emerged as a meticulous 14-day dry-ageing process. The monumental challenge extended beyond mastering the technique to solving the operational puzzle of consistently dry-ageing ducks in Hong Kong’s humid climate, a feat that ultimately required investing in a dedicated ageing fridge.
“What dry ageing does is essentially remove moisture from the skin, making it more consistently crispy,” Lau notes. This crucial step intensifies the flavour and firms the meat. The final, impossibly crisp, Peking duck-style crackle is then achieved through a process of brining, a maltose-vinegar treatment, and high-temperature roasting.

This meticulously prepared duck is accompanied by a suite of reimagined components. The classic orange is replaced by aged tangerine peel (chenpi), which Lau discovered in Hong Kong’s traditional markets. For the garnish, endives are slowly braised for 90 minutes with the chenpi, plus cinnamon and honey, while fresh endives provide a crisp textural contrast.
The sauce receives a vibrant kick from Himalayan Timut pepper, a relative of Sichuan pepper that provides a slight tingling sensation without any heat. The choice was deliberate: “The spiciness and numbness [of Sichuan pepper] don’t work well with the food we create at Épure,” Lau explains. “Timut pepper has similar characteristics but without that effect.” The result is an addictive, citrusy spice that keeps diners wanting more.
Completing the experience is a clear jasmine tea consommé crafted from the duck’s bones. Lau notes this was born from a sustainable ethos: “We did not want to waste any part of the duck.” The consommé also acts as a subtle nod to the Cantonese habit of drinking tea throughout a meal, offering a delicate, star anise-infused palate cleanser.

The presentation honours the whole duck in its full glory. The magnificent bird is first presented on a tray beautifully decorated with flowers before being carved in the kitchen. A portion is then served to each guest alongside a cup of consommé, with Timut pepper-spiked duck jus poured directly on the plate.
The spirit of reinvention that defines the duck already propels chef Lau towards his next challenge. His sights are set on another poultry, but his inspiration circles back to Asian ingredients, this time through the lens of a Mexican classic. “I am currently working on a new pigeon dish!” he reveals. “I envision creating an Asian-inspired mole poblano. Instead of chocolate, I’m incorporating a special black nut called buah keluak, along with fermented black beans. It will visually resemble a traditional mole, but its flavour profile will be distinctly Asian.”
For Lau, this relentless innovation is less about turning pages and more about adding depth to his culinary identity. “As chefs, we are always striving to perfect something or create something new. So, calling this a new chapter wouldn’t be accurate.”
“Rather, it’s a wonderful feeling to have a duck dish that I can call my own.”

Anatomy of a Dish
Name: Aged rice duck à l’orange with aged tangerine peel and Timut pepper
Restaurant: Épure
Price: Only available as part of the (dinner) tasting menu; HK$1,688
Main Ingredients: Guangdong rice duck, aged tangerine peel, Himalayan Timut pepper, endive, cinnamon, honey, star anise, Yunnan jasmine tea
Presentation: The whole bird makes a dramatic entrance, presented on a flower-adorned tray with rustic elegance before being carved in the kitchen. The final plating showcases a slice of crisp-skinned duck with perfectly rendered fat alongside the chenpi-infused endive, deliberately arranged to be enjoyed together. The aromatic Timut pepper sauce is poured tableside, while the small cup of jasmine tea consommé arrives separately.
Flavour: A masterful balance of deep umami and vibrant citrus. The rich flavour of the aged duck is accentuated by the sour-bitter complexity of the chenpi-braised endive and citrus gels, while the bright, electric buzz of the Timut pepper sauce creates an irresistibly moreish quality. The delicate jasmine tea consommé provides an aromatic interlude that cleanses the palate with subtle star anise, allowing the profound depth of the duck to linger beautifully before your next course.

data.title: The best outfits from All’s Fair – from Kim Kardashian in Dior to Naomi Watts in Valentino
data.description:
Ryan Murphy’s latest series, a female-led courtroom drama called All’s Fair on Hulu, features a high-profile cast that includes Kim Kardashian, Naomi Watts, Niecy Nash-Betts, Teyana Taylor, Glenn Close and Sarah Paulson.

For Murphy, who’s known for hits such as Glee, American Horror Story and Monster, the Kardashian-led show may be his most criticised work yet, after the show debuted with a rare zero per cent rating on Rotten Tomatoes after its premiere on November 4. The rating has since gone up to four per cent at the time of writing.
Critics may not be fans of the nine-part series (it was dubbed “fascinatingly, existentially terrible” by The Guardian), but the show’s fashion game is undeniably top tier.

From thong-bearing vintage Jean Paul Gaultier skirts to head-to-toe leopard prints by Valentino, here are some of All’s Fair’s best looks.

In one episode, Kim Kardashian’s character Allura Grant wears a Japanese floral-printed silk dress and coat from the Dior spring 2001 collection by John Galliano.

Watts, who plays Liberty Ronson, wears a leopard-print, faux fur caban jacket and an A-line skirt by Valentino.

Niecy Nash’s character, detective Emerald Greene, loves a good hat to go with her outfits. In one episode she rocks an all-blue outfit with a blazer from I Lyn Fashion Lounge. In her interview with The Zoe Report, the show’s costume designer Paula Bradley mentioned that Nash wanted to spotlight Black-owned brands.

Teyana Taylor plays Milan on the show, an aspiring lawyer with impeccable fashion taste. Here, she’s seen wearing a pink, long-sleeved top with a triangular cut-out by Rick Owens.

No one does minimalism and quiet luxury better than Liberty. In one scene, she pairs a white polo neck with a white double-breasted cashmere-corduroy blazer by Gabriela Hearst.

Kardashian and Watts’ characters share a close friendship on the show, despite their clashing wardrobes. This scene is a case in point. Where Allura goes for a bold red leather ensemble from Jean Paul Gaultier’s autumn/winter 2008 collection and Cartier sunnies, Liberty opts for a more muted, but still chic, grey wool cashmere cape by Carolina Herrera and a pair of Dior sunglasses.

In another scene, Emerald wears a zebra-striped blouse from Gucci paired with a pleated faux leather skirt from A.L.C.

Glenn Close, who plays Dina Standish on the show, wears a blue blazer by Dolce & Gabbana with floral motifs and a white pussy bow blouse.

Some might call it an unusual choice for a lawyer, but Kardashian’s character rocked this vintage Jean Paul Gaultier set with her thong peeking out in the show.

Milan wears a yellow Porterville Mountain sweatshirt and trousers by Rick Owens.

In another scene, Close’s character Dina wears Erdem’s Bridal Calla cloque coat.

Sarah Paulson, who plays All’s Fair’s snarky antagonist, Carrington Lane, looks particularly chic in one outfit from the show: a grey skirt suit from Dior.
